sets.title: A Teenage Inventor
paragraphs: At the age of fourteen, Jamie Chen from Singapore became interested in solving problems with technology. One day, his grandmother could not remember whether she had taken her daily medicine. Jamie decided to make a small device to help her. He spent three months learning to build a simple electronic alarm system.

The device was a small box that lit up and made a sound at the time his grandmother needed to take her medicine. It also had a button she could press to confirm she had taken it. If she forgot to press the button, it would remind her again thirty minutes later.

Jamie entered the invention in a school competition and came second. His science teacher encouraged him to develop the idea further and apply for a young inventors programme at a local university. Jamie is now working with engineering students there to improve his design and hopes to sell the product one day.

questions.prompt: Why did Jamie decide to make the device?
opts: He wanted to win a competition
He wanted to help his grandmother
questions.rationale: Jamie made the device because his grandmother had trouble remembering to take her medicine.

questions.prompt: How long did it take Jamie to build the device?
questions.rationale: The text says he spent three months learning to build the alarm system.

questions.prompt: What does the button on the device do?
opts: It confirms the medicine was taken
questions.rationale: The button lets his grandmother confirm she has taken her medicine.

questions.prompt: What result did Jamie get in the school competition?
opts: He did not finish the competition
questions.rationale: Jamie came second in the school competition.

questions.prompt: What is Jamie doing now?
opts: Working with university students to improve his design
questions.rationale: Jamie is working with engineering students at a local university to improve his design.

sets.title: School Garden Project
paragraphs: When Green Valley High School decided to turn an unused piece of land behind the sports hall into a garden, no one expected it to become such an important part of school life. The project started two years ago when a group of students proposed the idea to the school principal.

Today the garden produces vegetables, herbs and fruit which are used in the school canteen. Students from different year groups look after different sections of the garden. There is also a small greenhouse where seedlings are grown before being moved outside.

The garden has also become part of the school curriculum. Biology students study plants there, and geography students learn about soil types and weather. Students say the garden has helped them feel more connected to nature, and many have started growing plants at home too.

questions.prompt: Who first suggested the garden project?
questions.rationale: A group of students proposed the idea to the principal.

questions.prompt: What happens to the food grown in the garden?
opts: It is sold to local shops
It is used in the school canteen
questions.rationale: Vegetables, herbs and fruit from the garden are used in the school canteen.

questions.prompt: What is the greenhouse used for?
opts: Growing plants before they go outside
questions.rationale: The greenhouse is used to grow seedlings before they are moved outside.

questions.prompt: Which subjects use the garden for lessons?
questions.rationale: Biology and geography students use the garden as part of their curriculum.

questions.prompt: How has the garden affected students outside school?
opts: They spend less time on screens
Many have begun growing plants at home
questions.rationale: Many students have started growing plants at home too.

sets.title: The Young Chef
paragraphs: Elena Russo is sixteen and already runs her own small catering business. It started when she began helping her aunt prepare food for family celebrations. People tasted her cooking and asked her to cook for their events too. Now she gets several requests a month.

Elena prepares everything herself in her family's kitchen. She specialises in Italian food but has learnt to cook dishes from other countries too because clients often want variety. Her most popular dish is a type of pasta she invented herself.

Balancing school and work is not always easy. Elena does most of her cooking at weekends and sometimes during school holidays. Her parents help her with the practical side of running a business, such as managing orders and accounting. Elena says she wants to study at a professional cookery school after she finishes her education.

questions.prompt: How did Elena start her catering business?
opts: People liked her food and asked her to cook for them
Her school helped her set it up
questions.rationale: People tasted her cooking and asked her to cook for their events.

questions.prompt: Why has Elena learnt to cook dishes from other countries?
questions.rationale: She learnt other cuisines because clients often want variety.

questions.prompt: What is Elena's most popular dish?
opts: A pasta dish she created herself
questions.rationale: Her most popular dish is a pasta recipe she invented herself.

questions.prompt: When does Elena usually do her cooking?
questions.rationale: Elena does most of her cooking at weekends and during school holidays.

questions.prompt: What do Elena's parents help with?
questions.rationale: Her parents help with managing orders and accounting.

sets.title: A Very Long Walk
paragraphs: Last summer, fifteen-year-old Marcus and his father completed a 120 kilometre walk across a national park. It took them seven days. Marcus had never done anything like it before and was nervous at the beginning. His father, who had done long walks before, helped him prepare.

They trained for four months before the walk, going on shorter trips at weekends to build up strength and practise using their equipment. Marcus also learnt to read maps, which was a skill he had never needed before.

During the walk they passed through forests, over mountains and along rivers. Marcus says the hardest part was the third day, when it rained all day and the paths were very muddy. But he also says that finishing the walk was the proudest moment of his life so far. He is already planning his next challenge.

questions.prompt: How long did the walk take to complete?
questions.rationale: The walk took them seven days.

questions.prompt: Who had experience of long walks before?
questions.rationale: Marcus's father had done long walks before.

questions.prompt: What new skill did Marcus learn while preparing?
opts: How to set up a tent
questions.rationale: Marcus learnt to read maps, a skill he had never needed before.

questions.prompt: What was the most difficult part of the walk for Marcus?
opts: Walking in the rain on the third day
The last day when he was very tired
questions.rationale: The hardest part was the third day when it rained all day.

questions.prompt: How does Marcus feel about completing the walk?
opts: He is glad it is over
It was the proudest moment of his life so far
He does not want to do it again
questions.rationale: Marcus says finishing was the proudest moment of his life so far.

sets.title: Saving Pocket Money
paragraphs: When Jade was thirteen, she decided she wanted to buy herself a new laptop for school. Her parents said they could not afford to buy one for her. Instead of being disappointed, Jade decided to save up her pocket money and do extra chores around the house to earn more.

She made a plan and calculated that she needed to save for about eight months. She wrote down all her spending and cut back on things like cinema visits and buying snacks at school. She also took on a job delivering newspapers at weekends.

After ten months – a little longer than planned – Jade had enough money. She researched laptops carefully before buying one that was good quality but not too expensive. She says the experience taught her to be more careful with money and to think about what she really needs before spending.

questions.prompt: Why did Jade need to save money herself?
opts: She wanted to choose her own laptop
Her parents could not afford to buy one
The laptop was a birthday gift she wanted
questions.rationale: Her parents said they could not afford to buy a laptop for her.

questions.prompt: How long did Jade originally plan to save?
questions.rationale: She calculated she needed to save for about eight months.

questions.prompt: How did Jade reduce her spending?
opts: She stopped going out with friends
She cut back on cinema and snacks
questions.rationale: She cut back on cinema visits and buying snacks at school.

questions.prompt: What job did Jade do to earn extra money?
questions.rationale: She took on a job delivering newspapers at weekends.

questions.prompt: What did the experience teach Jade?
opts: To ask her parents for more pocket money
To think more carefully about spending
To choose expensive but high-quality products
questions.rationale: Jade says the experience taught her to be more careful with money and think about what she really needs.

sets.title: An Online Tutor
paragraphs: Fifteen-year-old Ava from Canada started tutoring other students online after lockdown. During that difficult period, she helped her younger brothers with their schoolwork and realised she was good at explaining things clearly. She then decided to offer her help to other students.

Ava mainly helps students aged ten to thirteen with maths and English. She has three regular students and charges a small fee per lesson. She prepares her lessons carefully, making worksheets and videos to explain difficult concepts.

Ava finds running her mini-business alongside school challenging but rewarding. The money she earns goes into a savings account she plans to use for university. She also says that tutoring has helped her own understanding of the subjects she teaches.

questions.prompt: What made Ava realise she was good at teaching?
opts: A teacher told her she was good at explaining
She helped her brothers and did it well
She got top marks in her exams
questions.rationale: She helped her younger brothers and realised she was good at explaining.

questions.prompt: Which students does Ava teach?
opts: Students the same age as her
Students who are younger than her
questions.rationale: She teaches students aged 10 to 13, who are younger than her.

questions.prompt: What materials does Ava make for her lessons?
questions.rationale: Ava makes worksheets and videos to explain concepts.

questions.prompt: What does Ava do with the money she earns?
opts: She gives it to her parents
questions.rationale: The money goes into a savings account she plans to use for university.

questions.prompt: How has tutoring helped Ava?
opts: It has helped her make more friends
It has improved her understanding of what she teaches
It has made school much easier for her
questions.rationale: Ava says tutoring has helped her own understanding of the subjects she teaches.

sets.title: A Green School
paragraphs: Hillside School in Wales decided two years ago to become more environmentally friendly. Students and teachers worked together to come up with ideas. The first change was to stop using single-use plastic in the canteen. Students now bring reusable water bottles and lunch boxes.

The school also put solar panels on the roof of the main building. These provide around 30% of the school's electricity. Any extra electricity the panels produce goes back to the local energy grid. The school has already saved a significant amount of money on electricity bills.

A student environmental committee meets once a month to discuss new ideas. Recent projects include planting a wildflower area on the school field and starting a composting system for food waste. Students say they feel proud of these changes and that it has made them more aware of environmental issues in daily life.

questions.prompt: What was the first environmental change the school made?
opts: Stopping the use of single-use plastic
questions.rationale: The first change was to stop using single-use plastic in the canteen.

questions.prompt: How much electricity do the solar panels produce?
questions.rationale: The panels provide around 30% of the school's electricity.

questions.prompt: What happens to extra electricity from the solar panels?
opts: It goes back to the local energy grid
It powers the school at night
questions.rationale: Extra electricity goes back to the local energy grid.

questions.prompt: How often does the student environmental committee meet?
questions.rationale: The committee meets once a month.

questions.prompt: How have the changes affected students?
opts: They are more aware of environmental issues
They have less homework to do
questions.rationale: Students say it has made them more aware of environmental issues.

sets.title: Learning to Fail
paragraphs: When fourteen-year-old Daniel failed his first music exam, he was very upset. He had practised hard but made too many mistakes on the day. His music teacher told him that failing was an important part of learning and that many great musicians had failed exams early in their careers.

Daniel decided not to give up. He changed his practice routine, working on his weaknesses instead of just playing the pieces he already knew well. He also started recording himself and listening back to spot mistakes more easily.

Six months later Daniel took the exam again and passed with a high mark. He now helps other students at his school who are nervous about exams. He tells them that failure is not the end and that understanding why you failed is more useful than just feeling bad about it.

questions.prompt: Why did Daniel fail his first music exam?
opts: He made too many mistakes on the day
questions.rationale: He had practised hard but made too many mistakes on the day.

questions.prompt: What did Daniel's teacher tell him about failing?
opts: Failing is an important part of learning
He should choose a different instrument
questions.rationale: His teacher said failing was an important part of learning.

questions.prompt: How did Daniel change his practice?
opts: He practised for longer hours every day
He focused on his weak areas instead of easy pieces
questions.rationale: He worked on his weaknesses instead of playing pieces he already knew.

questions.prompt: What new technique did Daniel use to find mistakes?
opts: He asked his teacher to watch him
He recorded himself and listened back
questions.rationale: He started recording himself and listening back to spot mistakes.

questions.prompt: What does Daniel do now to help other students?
opts: He gives them extra music lessons
He tells them that failure is not the end
He helps them choose exam pieces
questions.rationale: Daniel tells nervous students that failure is not the end.

sets.title: Life in a New Country
paragraphs: When Leila moved from Morocco to the UK at the age of thirteen, she could not speak much English. The first few months at her new school were very hard. She felt lonely and struggled to follow lessons. Her teachers were supportive and gave her extra help after school.

Leila decided to immerse herself in the language as much as possible. She watched English TV programmes, read simple books and tried to talk to classmates during breaks. Within a year her English was good enough to take part fully in all her classes.

Now, two years after arriving, Leila is one of the best students in her year. She has made many friends and is part of the school's student council. She wants to study law when she finishes school. She says that struggling with a new language taught her to be patient and to keep working even when things are difficult.

questions.prompt: How did Leila feel when she first arrived at her new school?
opts: Lonely and struggled to follow lessons
questions.rationale: She felt lonely and struggled to follow lessons.

questions.prompt: How did Leila's teachers help her?
opts: They spoke to her in French
They gave her extra help after school
They put her in a special class
questions.rationale: Her teachers were supportive and gave her extra help after school.

questions.prompt: What did Leila do to improve her English?
opts: She watched TV, read books and spoke to classmates
She joined a language class at weekends
questions.rationale: She watched TV, read books and tried talking to classmates.

questions.prompt: How long did it take Leila to take part fully in all her classes?
questions.rationale: Within a year her English was good enough to take part fully.

questions.prompt: What has Leila learnt from her experience?
opts: To ask for help when she needs it
To be patient and keep working when things are hard
To make friends quickly in a new place
questions.rationale: She says struggling taught her to be patient and keep working even when things are difficult.

sets.title: The School Radio Station
paragraphs: Three years ago, a group of students at Park Lane School set up a radio station that broadcasts through the school's speakers and online. The station, called PL Radio, plays music and includes shows about sport, school news and student interviews. It runs every day during the lunch break.

Any student can join and present a show after a short training session. Around twenty students currently take part. The most popular show is a weekly quiz where listeners can win small prizes. The station is run entirely by students with occasional advice from a media studies teacher.

The headteacher says the station has improved confidence and communication skills among students. Listeners say it makes the school day more enjoyable. The station has even won a regional award for the best school media project in its area.

questions.prompt: When does PL Radio broadcast?
opts: During the lunch break every day
questions.rationale: It runs every day during the lunch break.

questions.prompt: What does a student need to do before presenting a show?
questions.rationale: Students must complete a short training session before presenting.

questions.prompt: Which show is most popular with listeners?
questions.rationale: The most popular show is the weekly quiz.

questions.prompt: Who runs the station?
questions.rationale: The station is run entirely by students with occasional advice from a teacher.

questions.prompt: What award has the station received?
opts: Best school media project in the region
questions.rationale: The station won a regional award for best school media project in its area.

sets.title: A Young Photographer
paragraphs: Thirteen-year-old Sofia from Portugal became interested in photography after she found her grandfather's old film camera in the attic. She asked him to teach her how to use it, and he spent several weekends showing her basic techniques. Sofia loved the idea of capturing moments that would never happen again.

Sofia began taking photos around her neighbourhood and at school events. She entered a national youth photography competition with a picture she took of an elderly man feeding pigeons in a park. The judges liked the way she used light and shadow. She was one of fifty young photographers selected to take part in an exhibition.

After the competition, the school asked Sofia to take photos for the school website and newsletter. She is now saving money to buy her own digital camera. Sofia says photography has taught her to look at ordinary things more carefully and find beauty in everyday life.

questions.prompt: Where did Sofia find the camera she first used?
questions.rationale: She found her grandfather's old film camera in the attic.

questions.prompt: Who taught Sofia how to use the camera?
questions.rationale: Her grandfather spent several weekends showing her basic techniques.

questions.prompt: What did Sofia photograph for the competition?
opts: An elderly man feeding pigeons in a park
questions.rationale: She entered with a photo of an elderly man feeding pigeons in a park.

questions.prompt: What did the judges say about Sofia's photo?
opts: She used light and shadow well
questions.rationale: The judges liked the way she used light and shadow.

questions.prompt: What does Sofia do for her school now?
opts: She takes photos for the website and newsletter
questions.rationale: The school asked Sofia to take photos for the school website and newsletter.

sets.title: A Student Exchange Trip
paragraphs: Last spring, fifteen-year-old Tom from England spent two weeks in Germany as part of a school exchange programme. He stayed with a family in a small town near Munich. Tom was nervous before he left because his German was not very good, but the family were kind and patient with him.

During the two weeks, Tom went to school with his exchange partner, Max. He sat in on German, history and sport lessons. Some things were different from his school in England, such as the school day ending at lunchtime. He also tried local food for the first time, including dishes he had never heard of before.

When Tom returned home, he continued writing to Max by email. He also started practising his German more seriously. His teacher noticed a big improvement in his speaking skills. Tom says the trip helped him understand that people are friendly everywhere if you make an effort to communicate.

questions.prompt: Why was Tom nervous before the exchange trip?
opts: He had never been on a plane before
He did not speak much German
He did not know his exchange partner
questions.rationale: Tom was nervous because his German was not very good.

questions.prompt: What was one difference Tom noticed at the German school?
opts: The school day ended at lunchtime
Students did not have to wear a uniform
questions.rationale: He noticed the school day ended at lunchtime, which was different from England.

questions.prompt: What did Tom do after he returned home?
opts: He invited Max to visit England
He continued writing to Max by email
He joined a German language club
questions.rationale: Tom continued writing to Max by email after returning home.

questions.prompt: What improvement did Tom's teacher notice?
opts: His speaking skills had improved a lot
questions.rationale: His teacher noticed a big improvement in his speaking skills.

questions.prompt: What did Tom learn from the trip?
opts: That Germany is a beautiful country
That people are friendly everywhere if you try to communicate
That it is easy to learn a new language quickly
questions.rationale: Tom says the trip helped him understand that people are friendly everywhere if you make an effort to communicate.

sets.title: The School Newspaper
paragraphs: When sixteen-year-old Maya joined her school newspaper, it only had twelve readers. The paper was printed once a term and contained mostly sports results and announcements. Maya thought the newspaper could be much more interesting, so she spoke to the teacher in charge and offered to help redesign it.

Maya introduced a new section with interviews of interesting local people, including a retired firefighter and a chef who had worked in five countries. She also added a problem page where students could write in with questions about school life. The newspaper moved online, making it easier for everyone to read.

Within a year, the newspaper had over two hundred regular readers. Three other students joined the team to help write and design each issue. Maya won a regional young journalist award for her interview section. She says the experience has made her want to study journalism at university.

questions.prompt: What was the school newspaper like before Maya joined?
opts: It only contained sports and announcements
questions.rationale: The paper contained mostly sports results and announcements.

questions.prompt: What new section did Maya add to the newspaper?
opts: An interview section with local people
questions.rationale: Maya introduced a new section with interviews of interesting local people.

questions.prompt: What change did Maya make to how the newspaper was shared?
opts: She sold it in local shops
questions.rationale: The newspaper moved online, making it easier for everyone to read.

questions.prompt: How many regular readers did the newspaper have after one year?
questions.rationale: Within a year, the newspaper had over two hundred regular readers.

questions.prompt: What does Maya want to do in the future?
questions.rationale: She says the experience has made her want to study journalism at university.

sets.title: A Young Athlete
paragraphs: At the age of twelve, Kofi started running at a local athletics club after his PE teacher noticed he was faster than the other students in his year. At first Kofi was not very interested, but his coach, a former national champion, helped him see that he had real potential. Kofi began training three times a week after school.

Kofi entered his first regional competition eight months after joining the club. He was nervous and finished in fourth place. His coach told him that his technique needed work, especially his start. Kofi spent the next two months focusing on improving this part of his race.

A year later, Kofi won the regional under-15 100 metres title. His school gave him a special award at the end-of-year ceremony. Kofi says the most important lesson he has learnt is that natural ability is not enough — you also have to work very hard every single day.

questions.prompt: How did Kofi get started in athletics?
opts: He saw a competition on television
His PE teacher noticed he was fast and sent him to a club
His parents entered him for a competition
questions.rationale: His PE teacher noticed he was faster than others and he joined a local athletics club.

questions.prompt: How often did Kofi train after joining the club?
questions.rationale: Kofi began training three times a week after school.

questions.prompt: What position did Kofi finish in his first competition?
questions.rationale: He was nervous and finished in fourth place.

questions.prompt: What part of his race did Kofi work on after the first competition?
opts: His speed in the final stretch
questions.rationale: His coach said his start needed work, and Kofi focused on improving it.

questions.prompt: What lesson has Kofi learnt from athletics?
opts: That training with a good coach is the most important thing
That natural ability is not enough — hard work is also needed
That competitions are more important than training
questions.rationale: Kofi says natural ability is not enough — you also have to work very hard every day.

sets.title: Helping at a Food Bank
paragraphs: When fourteen-year-old Priya read an article about food banks in her city, she decided she wanted to do something to help. She found a local food bank that accepted volunteers and asked if she could help at weekends. The manager was happy to have her and explained how the food bank worked.

Every Saturday morning, Priya sorts food donations into boxes and helps pack them for families in need. She has learnt how to organise different types of food according to the date they should be used by. She also helps welcome people who come to collect food, making sure they feel comfortable.

Priya has now volunteered at the food bank for over a year. Several of her classmates have joined her. The manager has asked her to help train new volunteers. Priya says the experience has shown her how many people in her city need help, and she wants to work in the charity sector when she is older.

questions.prompt: Why did Priya decide to volunteer at the food bank?
opts: Her teacher told her to find volunteer work
She read an article about food banks
A friend invited her to join
questions.rationale: She read an article about food banks and decided she wanted to help.

questions.prompt: What does Priya do at the food bank on Saturday mornings?
opts: She sorts and packs food donations
She delivers food to people's homes
questions.rationale: She sorts food donations into boxes and helps pack them for families.

questions.prompt: What does Priya do when people come to collect food?
opts: She tells them what food is available
She helps them feel comfortable when they arrive
questions.rationale: She helps welcome people who come to collect food, making sure they feel comfortable.

questions.prompt: What has the manager asked Priya to do?
opts: To manage the food bank's social media
questions.rationale: The manager has asked her to help train new volunteers.

questions.prompt: What does Priya want to do when she is older?
questions.rationale: She says she wants to work in the charity sector when she is older.

sets.title: A Science Fair Winner
paragraphs: Twelve-year-old Amara had always been curious about science, but she never thought of herself as especially talented at it. When her school announced its annual science fair, her teacher encouraged her to enter. Amara decided to investigate whether plants grow better with music playing nearby — an idea she had heard about but wanted to test properly.

Amara set up a simple experiment at home using two groups of identical plants. She played classical music to one group and kept the other group in silence. She measured the height of each plant every three days for six weeks and wrote down all her results carefully. She also took photos to record the changes.

At the fair, the judges were impressed by her careful method and clear data. Amara came first in her age group and received a certificate and a book token. Her science teacher said her project was the most carefully designed she had seen from a student so young. Amara is now planning a new experiment about water quality.

questions.prompt: What was Amara's experiment about?
opts: Whether music helps plants grow better
Whether different soils affect plant height
questions.rationale: She wanted to test whether plants grow better with music playing nearby.

questions.prompt: How did Amara set up her experiment?
opts: She used one plant and measured it daily
She used two groups of plants — one with music, one without
She tested different types of music on the same plant
questions.rationale: She used two groups of identical plants — one with music and one in silence.

questions.prompt: How often did Amara measure her plants?
questions.rationale: She measured the height of each plant every three days.

questions.prompt: What did the judges think of Amara's project?
opts: The method was careful and the data was clear
questions.rationale: The judges were impressed by her careful method and clear data.

questions.prompt: What is Amara planning to do next?
opts: Enter the same experiment in another competition
Plan a new experiment about water quality
Write an article about her findings
questions.rationale: Amara is now planning a new experiment about water quality.

sets.title: Learning to Code
paragraphs: When thirteen-year-old Hassan wanted to create a game for his younger sister, he realised he needed to learn how to code. He found a free online course and started learning during evenings and weekends. His parents were worried he was spending too much time on his computer, but Hassan explained that he had a clear goal.

After three months of learning, Hassan built a simple memory card game using beginner-level programming language. The game had pictures of animals and played a short sound when a player matched two cards correctly. His sister loved it. Hassan shared it with his class and several students asked him to teach them what he had learnt.

Hassan then started a small coding club at school that meets once a week. Currently eight students attend. Hassan prepares simple exercises for each session and answers questions. His computer science teacher visits the club sometimes to give extra guidance. Hassan says coding has taught him that breaking a big problem into small steps makes everything easier.

questions.prompt: Why did Hassan decide to learn to code?
opts: His teacher set it as homework
He wanted to make a game for his sister
He wanted to win a competition at school
questions.rationale: He wanted to create a game for his younger sister.

questions.prompt: How did Hassan learn to code?
opts: He took lessons at a coding school
He used a free online course
A friend at school taught him
questions.rationale: He found a free online course and studied during evenings and weekends.

questions.prompt: What happened when a player matched two cards in Hassan's game?
opts: The game played a short sound
questions.rationale: The game played a short sound when a player matched two cards correctly.

questions.prompt: How many students currently attend Hassan's coding club?
questions.rationale: Currently eight students attend the club.

questions.prompt: What lesson has coding taught Hassan?
opts: That making games is not as hard as it looks
That breaking big problems into small steps makes things easier
That sharing what you know helps others learn faster
questions.rationale: Hassan says coding has taught him that breaking a big problem into small steps makes everything easier.

sets.title: A Young Musician
paragraphs: Fourteen-year-old Clara started learning the violin at the age of six. For the first few years she practised only when her parents reminded her. But when she was eleven, she heard a professional orchestra play live for the first time and decided she wanted to play at that level one day. From that moment she practised every day without being asked.

Clara now practises for ninety minutes each morning before school. She has lessons every week with a teacher who used to play in a national orchestra. Clara also plays in her school orchestra and in a youth orchestra that meets on Sunday afternoons. Playing with other musicians, she says, is completely different from practising alone.

Last month, Clara auditioned for a place on a prestigious summer music programme in Austria. She was one of six young violinists selected from over three hundred applicants across Europe. The programme lasts four weeks and includes masterclasses with famous musicians. Clara says she is both very excited and very nervous about going.

questions.prompt: What changed Clara's attitude to practising the violin?
opts: A teacher told her she had special talent
She heard a live orchestra and wanted to reach that level
She won her first competition at school
questions.rationale: After hearing a professional orchestra live, she decided she wanted to play at that level.

questions.prompt: How long does Clara practise each day?
questions.rationale: Clara practises for ninety minutes each morning before school.

questions.prompt: Where does Clara's violin teacher come from?
questions.rationale: Her teacher used to play in a national orchestra.

questions.prompt: How many young violinists were selected for the summer programme?
questions.rationale: She was one of six young violinists selected.

questions.prompt: How does Clara feel about the summer programme?
questions.rationale: Clara says she is both very excited and very nervous about going.

sets.title: Starting a Business at School
paragraphs: When sixteen-year-old Sam needed money to buy new sports equipment, his father suggested he think of a way to earn it himself. Sam noticed that many students at his school wanted personalised phone cases but could not find affordable ones in local shops. He decided to design and sell them himself.

Sam bought a heat-press machine second-hand and learnt to use design software online. He began by selling to friends, charging a fair price that covered his costs and left a small profit. Word spread quickly and he was soon getting five or six orders a week from students across the school.

After six months Sam had earned enough for his sports equipment and had money left over. His business studies teacher used Sam's business as a case study for the whole class. Sam says the experience taught him about pricing, customer service and managing time. He plans to continue the business part-time while he is at university.

questions.prompt: Why did Sam decide to start a business?
opts: He wanted to learn about business for school
He needed money to buy sports equipment
His teacher asked him to find an entrepreneurship project
questions.rationale: He needed money to buy new sports equipment.

questions.prompt: What problem did Sam notice at his school?
opts: Students wanted personalised phone cases but affordable ones were hard to find
Students needed help with their homework
There was no good place to buy school supplies
questions.rationale: He noticed students wanted personalised phone cases but could not find affordable ones locally.

questions.prompt: How did Sam learn to use the design software?
opts: He took a class at school
questions.rationale: He learnt to use design software online.

questions.prompt: How many orders a week was Sam getting after word spread?
questions.rationale: He was soon getting five or six orders a week.

questions.prompt: What did Sam's business studies teacher do with his story?
opts: She helped him improve his pricing
She used his business as a case study for the class
She encouraged him to enter a business competition
questions.rationale: His teacher used Sam's business as a case study for the whole class.

sets.title: A Young Writer
paragraphs: Fifteen-year-old Mia has always loved reading, but it was a creative writing competition at her school that turned her into a writer. Her English teacher asked students to write a short story in any genre they liked. Mia decided to write a mystery story set in her own town. She finished it in one weekend and was surprised by how much she enjoyed the process.

Mia won first place in the competition and her story was published in the school literary magazine. Encouraged by this, she began writing every day in a notebook she kept beside her bed. She started a second, longer story and shared chapters with a small group of friends who gave her feedback.

Earlier this year, Mia entered a national young writers competition and reached the final twenty from over two thousand entries. Although she did not win, one of the judges wrote to tell her that her writing showed real promise. Mia is now working on her third story and reads widely to improve her style.

questions.prompt: What made Mia start writing stories?
opts: She read a book that inspired her
A school competition gave her the chance to try
A friend suggested she write a story
questions.rationale: A creative writing competition at her school turned her into a writer.

questions.prompt: What type of story did Mia write for the competition?
questions.rationale: Mia decided to write a mystery story.

questions.prompt: Where was Mia's first story published?
questions.rationale: Her story was published in the school literary magazine.

questions.prompt: What did Mia do to get feedback on her second story?
opts: She sent it to her English teacher
She shared chapters with a small group of friends
She posted it on an online writing forum
questions.rationale: She shared chapters with a small group of friends who gave her feedback.

questions.prompt: What happened at the national young writers competition?
opts: Mia reached the final twenty but did not win
Mia did not pass the first round
questions.rationale: She reached the final twenty from over two thousand entries but did not win.

sets.title: An Eco-Friendly Project
paragraphs: Thirteen-year-old Isla was worried about the amount of rubbish she saw in the river near her school. She researched the problem and discovered that most of the rubbish came from plastic bottles and wrappers that people dropped in the streets. Isla decided to organise a monthly clean-up event with the help of her classmates.

The first clean-up had only eight participants, but Isla made a short video about it and posted it on the school's social media page. Within two months, the event had grown to over forty volunteers including parents and local residents. Isla worked with the local council to get equipment such as gloves and bags provided for free.

After six months, the amount of rubbish in the river had reduced noticeably. A local environmental charity invited Isla to speak at one of their events about how young people can make a difference. Isla says she was surprised to see how quickly things changed once people started working together.

questions.prompt: What problem did Isla want to solve?
opts: Rubbish in the river near her school
Damage to trees in the local park
questions.rationale: Isla was worried about the amount of rubbish in the river near her school.

questions.prompt: Where did most of the river rubbish come from, according to Isla's research?
opts: From plastic bottles and wrappers dropped in streets
questions.rationale: Most rubbish came from plastic bottles and wrappers that people dropped in the streets.

questions.prompt: How did the clean-up event grow to forty volunteers?
opts: Isla handed out leaflets in the town
Isla posted a video on the school's social media page
The local council advertised the event
questions.rationale: Isla posted a short video on the school's social media page, and the event grew.

questions.prompt: What did the local council provide for the clean-up?
questions.rationale: The local council provided equipment such as gloves and bags for free.

questions.prompt: What did Isla learn from the project?
opts: That rivers can clean themselves over time
That things can change quickly when people work together
That councils are slow to respond to young people
questions.rationale: Isla says she was surprised to see how quickly things changed once people started working together.

sets.title: A Young Swimmer
paragraphs: When eleven-year-old Hana joined her local swimming club, she could already swim but had never trained seriously. In her first few weeks she found the early morning sessions very hard. She had to get up at five-thirty every day before school and swim for ninety minutes. Many of the other swimmers had been training for years.

Hana's coach saw that she had a strong kick but needed to improve her arm technique. He gave her extra exercises to do after the main session. Hana followed the advice carefully and within six months she had dropped her personal best time for the fifty metres by three seconds.

Last year, Hana qualified for the national junior championships for the first time. She finished eighth in her event, which surprised many people as she had only been training seriously for two years. Her coach says that Hana's willingness to listen and work on her weaknesses is what sets her apart from other swimmers her age.

questions.prompt: What did Hana find difficult when she first joined the club?
opts: The early morning sessions before school
questions.rationale: She found the early morning sessions very hard — getting up at five-thirty every day.

questions.prompt: What did Hana's coach say she needed to improve?
questions.rationale: Her coach said she had a strong kick but needed to improve her arm technique.

questions.prompt: How much did Hana improve her fifty-metre time?
questions.rationale: She dropped her personal best time by three seconds.

questions.prompt: Where did Hana finish in the national junior championships?
questions.rationale: She finished eighth in her event.

questions.prompt: According to Hana's coach, what makes her stand out?
opts: Her natural speed in the water
Her willingness to listen and work on her weaknesses
Her ability to train early in the morning
questions.rationale: Her coach says her willingness to listen and work on her weaknesses sets her apart.

sets.title: Travelling by Train for the First Time
paragraphs: When fifteen-year-old Josh decided to visit his cousin in Edinburgh, his parents said he could travel alone for the first time. Josh had never taken a long train journey by himself. He planned the trip carefully, looking up departure times, buying his ticket online in advance, and making a note of which platforms he needed.

The journey from London to Edinburgh took about four and a half hours. Josh sat by the window and spent most of the time reading and watching the countryside change from flat fields to mountains and forests. At one point the train was delayed for twenty minutes, which made Josh slightly worried he might miss his connection, but the delay was short enough that he arrived on time.

Josh's cousin met him at Edinburgh station. Josh says the trip made him feel much more independent and confident. He had managed every part of the journey without any help. He is already planning a longer trip by himself next summer to visit a friend in another country.

questions.prompt: Why did Josh travel to Edinburgh?
opts: He was going on a school trip
He wanted to see a new city
questions.rationale: Josh decided to visit his cousin in Edinburgh.

questions.prompt: How did Josh prepare for the journey?
opts: He asked a travel agent to help him
He looked up times, bought a ticket and noted his platforms
He asked his parents to plan the trip for him
questions.rationale: He looked up departure times, bought his ticket online and made a note of his platforms.

questions.prompt: How long did the train journey take?
opts: About four and a half hours
questions.rationale: The journey from London to Edinburgh took about four and a half hours.

questions.prompt: Why did the train delay worry Josh?
opts: He was afraid he would arrive in Edinburgh at night
He thought he might miss his connection
He had not told his cousin what time he was arriving
questions.rationale: The delay made him slightly worried he might miss his connection.

questions.prompt: What is Josh planning for next summer?
opts: A train trip across the United Kingdom
A longer trip by himself to visit a friend abroad
A camping holiday with his cousin
questions.rationale: He is already planning a longer trip by himself next summer to visit a friend in another country.

sets.title: A Cooking Competition
paragraphs: Fourteen-year-old Ben entered a regional cooking competition for young people after seeing it advertised at school. Contestants had to prepare a two-course meal for two people using a limited list of ingredients. Ben had no professional training but had been cooking at home since he was ten, often helping his parents prepare meals at weekends.

On the day of the competition Ben was given one hour to complete both courses. He decided to make a vegetable soup followed by a pasta dish with a herb sauce. Halfway through, he realised he had put too much salt in the soup. He tried to fix it by adding more vegetables and a little cream, which improved the flavour.

The judges scored Ben's dishes on taste, presentation and creativity. He came third overall and received a trophy and a place at a one-day masterclass with a professional chef. Ben says the competition taught him to stay calm when things go wrong and to think quickly to solve problems.

questions.prompt: Where did Ben see the cooking competition advertised?
questions.rationale: Ben saw the competition advertised at school.

questions.prompt: How long had Ben been cooking at home?
questions.rationale: He had been cooking at home since he was ten.

questions.prompt: What problem did Ben have during the competition?
opts: He ran out of one ingredient
He put too much salt in the soup
His pasta dish was not cooked enough
questions.rationale: He realised halfway through that he had put too much salt in the soup.

questions.prompt: How did Ben fix the problem with the soup?
opts: He added more vegetables and a little cream
He asked a judge for advice
questions.rationale: He added more vegetables and a little cream, which improved the flavour.

questions.prompt: What did Ben win as a prize?
opts: A trophy and a place at a masterclass
A cash prize and a certificate
questions.rationale: He received a trophy and a place at a one-day masterclass with a professional chef.

sets.title: Raising Money for Charity
paragraphs: After learning in a geography lesson about the lack of clean water in some parts of the world, thirteen-year-old Chloe wanted to do something practical to help. She found a charity that built water wells in rural communities and decided to raise money for it. Chloe's first idea was to organise a sponsored silence at school.

Chloe asked her headteacher for permission to hold the event during one school day. Students who took part agreed not to speak during lessons, and teachers were happy to join in too. Chloe made collection forms and asked students to get sponsorship from family and friends before the day.

The sponsored silence raised over eight hundred pounds, more than double what Chloe had hoped for. The money was enough to help fund one well. The charity sent the school a letter and a photo of the completed well. Chloe says the project showed her that one person's idea, however simple, can make a real difference.

questions.prompt: What gave Chloe the idea to raise money for charity?
opts: She watched a documentary about clean water
She learnt about the problem in a geography lesson
A charity worker visited her school
questions.rationale: She learned about the lack of clean water in a geography lesson.

questions.prompt: What was Chloe's fundraising idea?
opts: A sponsored run around the school field
A bake sale at the school entrance
A sponsored silence during a school day
questions.rationale: Chloe organised a sponsored silence at school.

questions.prompt: Who gave Chloe permission to hold the event?
questions.rationale: Chloe asked her headteacher for permission.

questions.prompt: How much money did the sponsored silence raise?
questions.rationale: The sponsored silence raised over eight hundred pounds.

questions.prompt: What did the charity send to the school afterwards?
opts: An invitation to visit the well
A letter and a photo of the completed well
questions.rationale: The charity sent the school a letter and a photo of the completed well.

sets.title: Learning Sign Language
paragraphs: When a new student called Leo, who was deaf, joined her class, twelve-year-old Nadia wanted to be able to talk to him. She did not know any sign language, so she found a free beginner's course online and started learning in the evenings. After two weeks she knew enough basic signs to say hello and introduce herself to Leo.

Leo was pleased that Nadia had made the effort. He began teaching her more signs during lunchtime. They communicated a mix of sign language and written notes at first. Over the following months, Nadia's signing improved significantly and she began to help Leo communicate with other students who wanted to talk to him.

Nadia's story spread through the school, and a teacher set up a sign language club that now has eighteen members. Leo helps to lead the sessions alongside the teacher. Nadia says learning sign language has changed how she thinks about communication and has made her understand how isolating it can feel to be the only person in a room who communicates differently.

questions.prompt: Why did Nadia decide to learn sign language?
opts: It was required for a school project
She wanted to communicate with a deaf classmate
A teacher suggested it would be useful
questions.rationale: She wanted to be able to talk to Leo, a deaf student who joined her class.

questions.prompt: How did Nadia learn the basics of sign language?
opts: She took classes at a community centre
She used a free online course
questions.rationale: She found a free beginner's course online and started learning in the evenings.

questions.prompt: How did Nadia and Leo first communicate?
opts: A mix of sign language and written notes
questions.rationale: They communicated using a mix of sign language and written notes at first.

questions.prompt: How many members does the school sign language club now have?
questions.rationale: The sign language club now has eighteen members.

questions.prompt: What has Nadia learnt from her experience?
opts: That sign language is not as difficult as people think
How isolating it can feel to communicate differently from everyone else
That deaf people are very good at communicating in different ways
questions.rationale: She now understands how isolating it can feel to be the only person who communicates differently.

sets.title: A Young Blogger
paragraphs: Sixteen-year-old Imogen started her blog as a way of sharing her thoughts about the books she was reading. She did not tell anyone at first because she was not sure if her writing was good enough. After three months she had written fourteen posts and had a small number of regular readers who left encouraging comments.

Imogen's blog focuses on books for young adults and includes reviews, author interviews she conducts by email, and lists of recommended reading. She writes in a friendly and honest style, which her readers appreciate. A small publisher noticed her work and sent her some new books to review before their official release.

Imogen now has over three thousand followers and has been invited to two book festivals as a guest blogger. She uses her platform to recommend books that feature characters from different backgrounds, which she feels are often overlooked. Imogen says the blog has improved her writing and given her the confidence to share her opinions more freely.

questions.prompt: Why did Imogen not tell anyone about her blog at first?
opts: She wanted to surprise people when it was popular
She was not sure her writing was good enough
She was not allowed to share personal blogs at school
questions.rationale: She did not tell anyone because she was not sure if her writing was good enough.

questions.prompt: How does Imogen conduct author interviews for her blog?
opts: She meets authors at book festivals
She records video calls with them
questions.rationale: She conducts author interviews by email.

questions.prompt: Why did a publisher send Imogen books?
opts: They wanted her to review the books before release
She asked them for free copies
questions.rationale: A publisher noticed her work and sent her books to review before their official release.

questions.prompt: How many followers does Imogen's blog now have?
questions.rationale: Imogen now has over three thousand followers.

questions.prompt: What types of books does Imogen especially recommend?
opts: Books featuring characters from different backgrounds
questions.rationale: She recommends books that feature characters from different backgrounds, which she feels are often overlooked.

sets.title: A School Pet Project
paragraphs: When a group of students at Riverside Primary suggested getting a classroom pet, most people expected the idea to be rejected. But the school agreed, on condition that the students presented a proper plan for looking after the animal. Fourteen-year-old Oliver, who was in the school's junior leadership team, took charge of the project.

Oliver researched several animals and presented a plan for keeping two guinea pigs. The plan covered feeding, cleaning the cage, and veterinary care. He divided the responsibilities among students from two classes, making a rota so that every student had a turn. He also contacted a local vet, who agreed to answer questions and check on the animals once a term.

The guinea pigs arrived in September and quickly became popular with the whole school. Teachers noticed that younger students in particular became calmer and more focused during lessons after spending time with the animals. Oliver says the project taught him how important it is to plan carefully before starting something new.

questions.prompt: What did the school ask students to do before agreeing to a class pet?
opts: Get parents to sign a permission form
Present a proper plan for caring for the animal
Raise money to buy the animal themselves
questions.rationale: The school agreed on condition that students presented a proper plan for looking after the animal.

questions.prompt: What animals did Oliver suggest getting?
questions.rationale: Oliver presented a plan for keeping two guinea pigs.

questions.prompt: How did Oliver organise who would care for the animals?
opts: He asked for volunteers each week
He made a rota so every student had a turn
He chose a team of six students to be responsible
questions.rationale: He made a rota so that every student had a turn.

questions.prompt: How often did the local vet visit the animals?
questions.rationale: The vet agreed to check on the animals once a term.

questions.prompt: What effect did the guinea pigs have on younger students?
opts: They were excited and found it harder to concentrate
They became calmer and more focused during lessons
They spent too much time in the room with the animals
questions.rationale: Teachers noticed that younger students became calmer and more focused after spending time with the animals.

sets.title: A New Sports Club
paragraphs: When seventeen-year-old Fatima moved to a new town, she wanted to find a sport to help her meet people. She tried basketball and swimming but did not enjoy them. A friend suggested ultimate frisbee, a team sport she had never heard of. Fatima found a club forty minutes away and decided to go along to a taster session.

She enjoyed it immediately. The sport involves throwing a frisbee between team members to score goals, and the rules do not allow physical contact. Fatima liked the fact that matches relied more on strategy and communication than strength. Within two months she had become a regular member of the team.

Fatima noticed that there was no ultimate frisbee club at her school, so she asked the PE department if she could start one. A teacher agreed to supervise. Fatima designed a poster, gave a short presentation in assembly and recruited twelve students. The club now meets every Tuesday after school and has entered a local schools competition.

questions.prompt: Why did Fatima want to find a sport when she moved to the new town?
opts: She wanted to stay fit after stopping swimming
She needed a sport for her school timetable
questions.rationale: She wanted to find a sport to help her meet people.

questions.prompt: How did Fatima find out about ultimate frisbee?
opts: She saw a match on television
questions.rationale: A friend suggested ultimate frisbee to her.

questions.prompt: What does Fatima like about ultimate frisbee?
opts: It is easy to learn quickly
It relies more on strategy and communication than strength
The equipment is very cheap to buy
questions.rationale: She liked that it relied more on strategy and communication than strength.

questions.prompt: How did Fatima recruit students for the school club?
opts: She handed out leaflets at lunchtime
She gave a short presentation in assembly
She asked friends to invite their classmates
questions.rationale: She designed a poster, gave a short presentation in assembly and recruited twelve students.

questions.prompt: When does the school ultimate frisbee club meet?
questions.rationale: The club meets every Tuesday after school.

sets.title: A Young Environmentalist
paragraphs: Fifteen-year-old Zara became passionate about the environment after her class visited a nature reserve where the guide explained that several local species of bird were disappearing because of habitat loss. That evening Zara read more about the problem and felt she had to do something. She started by writing a letter to her local council asking what plans they had to protect green spaces.

The council replied and invited Zara to attend a public meeting about a planned development near the nature reserve. Zara prepared carefully. She brought photographs, maps and information about the birds at risk. When she spoke at the meeting, the audience listened carefully. Two local journalists reported on what she said.

As a result of Zara's campaign, the council agreed to change the development plan to protect a section of the habitat. An environmental group contacted Zara and asked her to become a youth ambassador. Zara says she is proof that young people can influence decisions that affect the environment if they are prepared and persistent.

questions.prompt: What made Zara become interested in environmental issues?
opts: She watched a film about endangered animals
A guide at a nature reserve explained how birds were disappearing
Her science teacher set a project on habitat loss
questions.rationale: A guide at a nature reserve explained that local birds were disappearing because of habitat loss.

questions.prompt: What did Zara do first after visiting the nature reserve?
opts: She organised a protest outside the council offices
She wrote a letter to her local council
questions.rationale: She started by writing a letter to her local council asking about their plans for green spaces.

questions.prompt: What did Zara bring to the public meeting?
opts: Photos, maps and information about birds at risk
A video she had made about the nature reserve
questions.rationale: She brought photographs, maps and information about the birds at risk.

questions.prompt: What was the result of Zara's campaign?
opts: The council changed the plan to protect part of the habitat
The council agreed to create a new nature reserve
questions.rationale: The council agreed to change the development plan to protect a section of the habitat.

questions.prompt: What does Zara say about young people and the environment?
opts: Young people should leave environmental decisions to experts
Young people can influence decisions if they are prepared and persistent
Young people need to vote before they can make a difference
questions.rationale: Zara says young people can influence decisions if they are prepared and persistent.
